nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reef slopes rich in hydroids and anemones, at depths of 3–30 m

diet.notes: An obligate corallivore in the wild, feeding predominantly on coral polyps and small invertebrates. Generally considered very difficult to sustain in captivity; in most cases it requires a large, established reef with fast-growing coral colonies. Some aquarists have reported success supplementing with live mussels in the shell, but long-term survival without access to coral polyps tends to be poor.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3282x2462. Tack-sharp full-body profile of a vibrant bright-yellow adult with the diagnostic bold black oval spot below the dorsal fin and black eye bar clearly shown; best overall.
Rickard Zerpe; 2041x1531. Sharp, well-lit lateral shot of a bright-yellow individual with clear oval spot and eye bar over a reef background; strong alternate angle.
Jenny (JennyHuang); 2453x1665. Dramatic spot-lit portrait against dark water showing the black oval spot and eye bar; adds visual variety though lighting is high-contrast.

summary: The Mirror Butterflyfish (Chaetodon speculum) is a bright-yellow reef species with a bold black spot beneath the dorsal fin. It is generally considered expert-only, as it feeds almost exclusively on coral polyps in the wild and tends to decline in captivity without access to a thriving reef.
careNotes: The Mirror Butterflyfish is widely regarded as one of the most challenging butterflyfishes to keep in captivity. It is an obligate corallivore — a species that feeds almost exclusively on coral polyps in the wild — and tends to refuse or poorly accept prepared and frozen foods. Some aquarists have documented limited success using live mussels with crushed shells as a supplement, but long-term survival without access to live coral polyps has been reported as uncommon. A large tank (300 L or more) with a significant, thriving coral colony and ample open swimming space is generally advised. The fish is not reef-safe and has been known to damage or consume coral polyps and may nip at Tridacna clams. It tends to show some aggression toward conspecifics but is generally peaceful with other species. The Mirror Butterflyfish has been documented forming monogamous pair bonds in the wild, and acquiring an established pair is generally advised over keeping a single specimen.
breedingNotes: Reproduction in captivity has not been documented. In the wild, the Mirror Butterflyfish is oviparous — it releases eggs into the water column during spawning — and forms lasting monogamous pair bonds through the breeding season. Captive breeding is considered impractical under typical aquarium conditions, given how rarely the species can be sustained long-term.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (18 cm TL vs LiveAquaria's 6 in/~15 cm), temperature range (FishBase 25–29.3°C vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F/22–26°C), taxonomy (family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes), native range, habitat, diet (coral polyps and invertebrates), and reproductive behavior (monogamous pair-bonding confirmed). Temperature min averaged to 24°C, max to 28°C. Adult size max taken from FishBase (18 cm); min set conservatively at 12 cm.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with monogamous pair-bonding in the wild; captive breeding is not documented and considered impractical given dietary requirements.
